rulings.Case Title: CAVALRY SPV I, LLC. VS. BRIAN J JONES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order: Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, September 24, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF CAVALRY SPV I, LLC. Motion For Order: Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Parties will be e-mailed a tentative ruling. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highmanlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KAZUKO K. ARTUS VS. GRAMERCY TOWERS CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 425.16
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 24, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT GRAMERCY TOWERS CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION's Motion For Attorneys Fees Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 425.16 Defendant Gramercy Towers Condominium Association's motion for attorneys' fees is granted in part. GTCA is awarded $10,000 for the fees that are "reasonably allocable to achieving" its "victory" in striking the fifth cause of action. (Area 51 Productions, Inc. v. City of Alameda (2018) 20 Cal. App. 5th 581, 605). Based on the court's recall of the anti-SLAPP motion and review of the papers filed on this motion, $10,000 is a reasonable amount of fees that were incurred by GTCA on the sole cause of action it prevailed on its anti-SLAPP motion and for seeking fees for that limited victory. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for GTCA is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: JEAN KASEM VS. MICHAEL D. HUNTER MD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 24, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT MICHAEL HUNTER, MD's Motion For Leave To File Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint Defendant Michael Hunter's motion for leave to file an anti-SLAPP motion to strike is denied. Dr. Hunter has failed to show good cause to support his request to file an anti-SLAPP motion at this point in the case. Dr. Hunter did not provide any admissible evidence in his moving papers. The asserted reason for the delay lacks merit. There is nothing improper with calendaring an anti-SLAPP motion against a pro per litigant. Allowing the filing of an anti-SLAPP motion at this stage of the case undermines rather than advances the goals of the anti-SLAPP statute. The prior demurrers and negotiations regarding Dr. Hunter's deposition would have been averted by an earlier anti-SLAPP motion assuming the motion was successful. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mrs. Kasem is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: ELECTRICAL INDUSTRY SERVICE BUREAU, INC. VS. THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 24, 2018, Line 9, Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company's Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company's demurrer to both causes of action alleged in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Electrical Industry Service Bureau, Inc. is sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for failure to pay on a payment bond and overruled as to the second cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law. The prior tentative ruling overruling the demurrer to the second cause of action was confirmed at the September 4, 2108 hearing. At that hearing the demurrer to the first cause of action was continued to allow the parties to provide further briefing on equitable tolling. Review of that further briefing shows that EISB's equitable tolling argument lacks merit as a matter of law. EISB filed this lawsuit after the expiration of the limitations period for a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond. EISB's effort to be relieved of its untimely first cause of action based on equitable tolling is unavailing. EISB has not alleged that it filed a "claim" prior to filing of this lawsuit. Thus, for instance, in Structural Steel Fabricators, Inc. v. City of Orange (1995) 40 Cal. App. 4th 449, the "first claim" was a prior lawsuit. Nor does McDonald v. Antelope Valley Community College District (2008) 45 Cal. 4th 88 support EISB's equitable tolling argument. The internal administrative remedy involved in McDonald had adjudicative qualities to it unlike EISB's submission of a claim to Travelers. Moreover, federal case law cited in Travelers' supplemental memorandum shows that the federal courts that have considered the same or substantially the same equitable tolling argument made by EISB have rejected that argument. In the absence of any controlling California authority, this court finds that federal case law persuasive. Liberally construed, the allegations in the FAC suggest that EISB may be seeking to invoke equitable estoppel as a basis to avoid the statute of limitations governing a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond, notwithstanding that EISB does not mention equitable estoppel in either the FAC or in its opposition memorandum. Nonetheless, EISB's allegations fall far short of what is required to adequately plead equitable estoppel. (Lantzy v. Centex Homes (2003) 31 Cal. 4th 363, 385). Citing Krantz v. BT Visual Images, L.L.C. (2001) 89 Cal. App. 4th 164, Travelers's sole argument in its initial memorandum as to the UCL claim is that the UCL claim fails because the payment bond claim failed. Not so. Unlike this case, Krantz did not involve the failure of an "antecedent claim" based on the statute of limitations. Per Business and Professions Code 17208, the UCL has its own statute of limitations and that four year limitations period, not the limitations period of the "antecedent" claim, generally governs the UCL claim. (Cortez v. Purolator Air Filtration Products Co. (2000) 23 Cal 4th 163, 178-79 (four year limitations period applies to UCL claim based on a violation of the Labor Code even though the Labor Code had a shorter limitations period)); see also Business and Professions Code 17205 (UCL claims are cumulative of claims under other California laws)). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Travelers is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=302/hk

rulings.Case Title: CALIFORNIA UNITED MECHANICAL, INC. VS. MW LOGIC, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 24, 2018, Line 10, DEFENDANTs 22 BATTERY STREET, INC. and HYPERBARIC MEDICAL SERVICES, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication Defendants Hyperbaric Medical Services LLC and 22 Battery Street, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendants' alternate motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the second cause of action for foreclosure of mechanic's line and granted as to the third and fourth causes of action for common count and quantum meruit. Defendants have not shown that they are entitled to prevail on the mechanic's lien claim because: (1) there is a triable dispute when the work was completed (Fox Dec., par.5), (2) the proof of service that defendants cite to argue that the preliminary notice was served in an untimely fashion does not refer to a "preliminary notice"; and (3) the alleged preliminary notice that is purportedly substantively deficient is clearly a document that is unrelated to this case. The court does not address the new arguments that defendants raise in their reply brief. Defendants are entitled to prevail on the common count and quantum meruit claims per Truestone, Inc. v. Simi West Industrial Park (1984) 163 Cal.App.3d 715, 724. The undisputed facts show that defendants did not communicate with or mislead United Mechanical and they were not unjustly enriched since Hyperbaric paid MW Logic, Inc. for the work. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for movants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk
